question_text: Water has a boiling point of 100°C at sea level. Which of the following best explains why water boils at a lower temperature when heated at high altitude (on a mountain)?

options.label: A
options.text: The air temperature is lower at high altitude, so water molecules move more slowly.

options.label: B
options.text: Atmospheric pressure is lower at high altitude, so water molecules can escape from the liquid more easily, requiring less energy.

options.label: C
options.text: Water contains fewer dissolved gases at high altitude, which reduces the boiling point.

options.label: D
options.text: The gravitational pull is weaker at high altitude, allowing water to boil more easily.

explanation: Boiling occurs when water vapour pressure equals atmospheric pressure. At lower atmospheric pressure (high altitude), water molecules need less kinetic energy to escape the liquid and form a gas, so boiling happens at a lower temperature. This demonstrates the relationship between pressure and boiling point.
